author: @viktorbozicevic6219
text:
Like the idea and the design mate! 
I have been using a balanced arm desk lamp for almost 11 years now, when it originally came with an E27 bulb socket intended for U-shaped energy saving bulbs. I later opted for a more environmentally and energy friendly LED alternative, but since quality LED bulbs are still expensive, I had to burn a couple of cheep Chinese LED lightbulbs in order to finally come up with an LED strip version of an existing desk lamp. Found this solution to be a little more expensive, given the length to brightness ratio of the LED strip, but nevertheless free of any unexpected burning out. Ditched the original lightbulb socked and the plastic shade, that was replaced by a stainless steel bowl turned upside down as a light reflector, and attached it to the original holder. Cut the neutral white LED strip to shorter pieces so they can fit onto a straight round plate of somewhat smaller diameter than the lampshade. Soldered all the strips in parallel and sticked them to a plate, even adding a dimmer module with a rotating switch-pot. Recycled a milky-white plastic bag that I used as a light diffuser and hot glued it in place (though my only concern is the slight heating of this part and eventual off gasing when LEDs operate on max brightness). 
Using the separate 12V DC power supply outside of the unit, and this lamp draws around 14W when on max power, but lights my desk perfectly for drawing and reading.

author: @rwhitenz
text: Yuck Hotmelt is for temporary stuff unless you have a high temperature one.  I would have used epoxy and maybe hotmelt to temporarily hold it in place while the epoxy set.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good idea mate - cheers

replies.author: @mickybee3247
replies.text: My thoughts entirely, but watch out for epoxy/glass temp co differentials – I've had glass fracture for that reason! You can make neat hole shapes in glass, using this method.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome mate - thanks for that info

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Neat chemical heating with structural reinforcement!

author: @CoffeeandCancerStix
text: I have a question, I noticed what appears to be a metal  ring on the bottle after you cut it, but didn't catch what it was. Was that  also something you found and upcycled or was it like some strips of aluminium that you formed? I only ask because cleaning up the edges of cut glass in a nice way can be a real pain to make look good.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
